caption: May 5, 1947: Environmental controls — humidity, heat, cold, and improper storage — caused the paper sleeves to bond to the lacquer, along with palmitic acid exudation.
May 5, 1947: Environmental controls — humidity, heat, cold, and improper storage — caused the paper sleeves to bond to the lacquer, along with palmitic acid exudation. (Web-resolution copy.)

May 5, 1947: Removing the paper should be slow and methodical, not pulling lacquer or damaging grooves. The key is to brush a light film of mineral oil onto the paper, wait, then easily remove the paper from the disc.
May 6, 1947: Side view of the acid buildup combined with possible mold throughout the grooves.
May 6, 1947: Another view showing the damage to the disc.
May 6, 1947: Close-up of non-grooved areas. The disc was rinsed with a distilled water and vinegar mix, applied and removed quickly so as not to damage the lacquer. This method is not effective.
May 6, 1947 (Disc #4): After the vinegar method and rinse, the full Pryse OHS Method was applied and cleaned the disc entirely.
May 5, 1947: View of cleaned disc on the transcription turntable, grooves recovered for playback.
May 5, 1947: Raking-light detail of cleaned groove field after full treatment.
May 6, 1947: View of cleaned disc mounted for transfer.
May 6, 1947: Label detail post-treatment — St. Luke's Methodist Church, Municipal Auditorium, Disc #4.
